claims.proposition: First Amendment protections apply to state and local governments, including police, through the Fourteenth Amendment.
claims.brief_sentence: These protections apply to state and local government—including the Chicago Police—through the Fourteenth Amendment. Edwards v. South Carolina, 372 U.S. 229, 235 (1963).

claims.proposition: The First Amendment's core purpose is to protect political dissent, particularly speech directed at government policy.
claims.cited_for: Political protest and boycotts are protected even when government policy is the target.
claims.cited_case: NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co., 458 U.S. 886, 907–09 (1982)
claims.brief_sentence: The Supreme Court has repeatedly held that the First Amendment's core purpose is to protect political dissent, particularly speech directed at government policy. NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co., 458 U.S. 886, 907–09 (1982) (political protest and boycotts protected even when government policy is the target).

claims.proposition: Streets and parks are traditional public forums that have immemorially been held in trust for public assembly and discussion, establishing the public forum doctrine.
claims.cited_case: Hague v. Committee for Industrial Organization, 307 U.S. 496, 515 (1939)
quoted_text: Streets and parks … have immemorially been held in trust for the use of the public and, time out of mind, have been used for purposes of assembly, communicating thoughts between citizens, and discussing public questions.
claims.brief_sentence: As Justice Roberts wrote in Hague v. Committee for Industrial Organization, 307 U.S. 496, 515 (1939) — the foundational case establishing the public forum doctrine: "Streets and parks … have immemorially been held in trust for the use of the public and, time out of mind, have been used for purposes of assembly, communicating thoughts between citizens, and discussing public questions."

claims.proposition: Content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions are valid only if content-neutral, narrowly tailored to a significant governmental interest, and leaving open ample alternative channels.
claims.cited_case: Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781, 791 (1989)
claims.brief_sentence: Under the standard articulated in Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781, 791 (1989), and Clark v. Community for Creative Non-Violence, 468 U.S. 288, 293 (1984), even content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions are constitutionally valid only if they are: [content-neutral; narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest; and leave open ample alternative channels].

claims.proposition: Content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions are valid only if content-neutral, narrowly tailored to a significant governmental interest, and leaving open ample alternative channels.
claims.cited_case: Clark v. Community for Creative Non-Violence, 468 U.S. 288, 293 (1984)
claims.brief_sentence: Under the standard articulated in Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781, 791 (1989), and Clark v. Community for Creative Non-Violence, 468 U.S. 288, 293 (1984), even content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions are constitutionally valid only if they are: [content-neutral; narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest; and leave open ample alternative channels].

claims.proposition: A content-based restriction triggers strict scrutiny, and even content-neutral dispersal orders must satisfy each of the three time, place, and manner prongs.
claims.cited_case: Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781, 791 (1989)
claims.brief_sentence: A content-based restriction triggers strict scrutiny and is presumptively unconstitutional. Even content-neutral dispersal orders must satisfy each of these three prongs. Ward, 491 U.S. at 791.

claims.proposition: The Fourteenth Amendment does not permit a State to make criminal the peaceful expression of unpopular views.
quoted_text: The Fourteenth Amendment does not permit a State to make criminal the peaceful expression of unpopular views.
claims.brief_sentence: The Supreme Court's decision in Edwards v. South Carolina, 372 U.S. 229 (1963), is strikingly on point. [...] The Supreme Court unanimously reversed, holding: "The Fourteenth Amendment does not permit a State to make criminal the peaceful expression of unpopular views."

claims.proposition: Speech retains First Amendment protection even when it induces unrest or stirs anger, and only a clear and present danger of a serious substantive evil rising far above public inconvenience can justify suppression.
quoted_text: induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger
clear and present danger of a serious substantive evil
rises far above public inconvenience, annoyance, or unrest
claims.brief_sentence: The Court stated that speech retains First Amendment protection even when it "induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger." Id. at 237 (quoting Terminiello v. City of Chicago, 337 U.S. 1 (1949)). Only a "clear and present danger of a serious substantive evil" — one that "rises far above public inconvenience, annoyance, or unrest" — can justify suppression of otherwise protected assembly.

claims.proposition: Speech retains First Amendment protection even when it induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction, or stirs people to anger.
claims.cited_for: Speech retains First Amendment protection even when it induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger.
claims.cited_case: Terminiello v. City of Chicago, 337 U.S. 1 (1949)
quoted_text: induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger
claims.brief_sentence: The Court stated that speech retains First Amendment protection even when it "induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger." Id. at 237 (quoting Terminiello v. City of Chicago, 337 U.S. 1 (1949)).

claims.proposition: Peaceful demonstrators' own conduct cannot form the basis of a criminal conviction, even where hostile bystanders threw rocks and eggs and police feared a riot.
claims.cited_case: Gregory v. City of Chicago, 394 U.S. 111 (1969)
claims.brief_sentence: Similarly, in Gregory v. City of Chicago, 394 U.S. 111 (1969), the Supreme Court unanimously reversed disorderly conduct convictions of peaceful civil rights protesters in Chicago who marched to Mayor Daley's home, even though hostile bystanders threw rocks and eggs and police feared an imminent riot. The Court held that the demonstrators' own peaceful conduct could not form the basis of a criminal conviction.

claims.proposition: Allowing a policeman's command to become equivalent to a criminal statute comes dangerously near making government one of men rather than of laws.
claims.cited_case: Gregory v. City of Chicago, 394 U.S. 111, 125 (1969)
quoted_text: To let a policeman's command become equivalent to a criminal statute comes dangerously near making our government one of men rather than of laws.
claims.brief_sentence: Justice Black's concurrence emphasized: "To let a policeman's command become equivalent to a criminal statute comes dangerously near making our government one of men rather than of laws." Id. at 125.

claims.proposition: Police may not disperse a peaceful protest simply because a hostile crowd reacts negatively; the heckler's veto is constitutionally prohibited.
claims.cited_case: Gregory v. City of Chicago, 394 U.S. 111, 125 (1969)
claims.brief_sentence: This is the forbidden "heckler's veto." Gregory v. City of Chicago, 394 U.S. at 125 (Black, J., concurring); Goldhamer v. Nagode, 621 F.3d 581 (7th Cir. 2010).

claims.proposition: Police may not disperse a peaceful protest simply because a hostile crowd reacts negatively; the heckler's veto is constitutionally prohibited.
claims.brief_sentence: This is the forbidden "heckler's veto." Gregory v. City of Chicago, 394 U.S. at 125 (Black, J., concurring); Goldhamer v. Nagode, 621 F.3d 581 (7th Cir. 2010).

claims.proposition: Chicago Municipal Code § 8-4-010(d) cannot be applied as a heckler's veto, and arrests of peaceful leafletters were apparently without probable cause.
claims.brief_sentence: In Goldhamer v. Nagode, the Seventh Circuit addressed a nearly identical situation [...]. The court held that Chicago Municipal Code § 8-4-010(d) cannot be applied as a heckler's veto and that the arrests were apparently without probable cause. 621 F.3d at 588–90.

claims.proposition: Illinois disorderly conduct requires that conduct both alarm others and provoke an actual, not merely speculative, breach of the peace.
claims.brief_sentence: Courts require that the conduct both alarm others and provoke an actual, not merely speculative, breach of peace. Thayer v. Chiczewski, 705 F.3d 237, 248–49 (7th Cir. 2012).

claims.proposition: Peaceful picketing, placard-carrying, and chanting cannot form the basis of disorderly conduct charges.
claims.brief_sentence: Moreover, as Illinois courts have held and the Seventh Circuit confirmed, peaceful picketing, placard-carrying, and chanting cannot form the basis of disorderly conduct charges. Id.

claims.proposition: A sidewalk gathering or press conference requires no parade permit, as senior police officers testified under oath.
claims.brief_sentence: In Thayer v. Chiczewski, 705 F.3d at 247, both senior police officers testified under oath that a sidewalk press conference requires no permit at all.

claims.proposition: The Seventh Circuit partially struck down Chicago Municipal Code § 8-4-010(d) as facially overbroad and unconstitutionally vague, severing the inconvenience/annoyance/alarm triggers and upholding only the substantial harm trigger.
claims.brief_sentence: However, the Seventh Circuit in Bell v. Keating, 697 F.3d 445 (7th Cir. 2012), partially struck down § 8-4-010(d) as facially overbroad and unconstitutionally vague. The court: severed and invalidated the "serious inconvenience, annoyance or alarm" triggers; upheld only the "substantial harm" trigger as constitutionally valid.

claims.proposition: Avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech.
quoted_text: avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech
claims.brief_sentence: The court held that "avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech," citing Coates v. City of Cincinnati, 402 U.S. 611 (1971). Bell v. Keating, 697 F.3d at 454.

claims.proposition: Avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech.
claims.cited_for: Avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech.
claims.cited_case: Coates v. City of Cincinnati, 402 U.S. 611 (1971)
quoted_text: avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech
claims.brief_sentence: The court held that "avoiding annoyance is never a proper basis on which to curtail protected speech," citing Coates v. City of Cincinnati, 402 U.S. 611 (1971). Bell v. Keating, 697 F.3d at 454.

claims.proposition: The 'alarm' and 'annoyance' terms were struck as both overbroad and unconstitutionally vague for giving officers standardless discretion and failing to give adequate notice.
claims.brief_sentence: The "alarm" and "annoyance" terms were struck as both overbroad (encumbering substantially more constitutionally protected expression than legitimate conduct) and unconstitutionally vague (providing officers with standardless discretion and failing to give citizens adequate notice). Id. at 455–58.

claims.proposition: The court upheld the authority to issue a dispersal order where 8,000 protesters occupied arterial roadways and the march was getting out of hand.
claims.cited_case: Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, 744 (7th Cir. 2011)
quoted_text: getting out of hand
claims.brief_sentence: Even in Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, 744 (7th Cir. 2011) — where the court upheld the authority to issue a dispersal order — the justification involved 8,000 protesters occupying major arterial roadways, some marchers banging on cars in rush-hour traffic, and shouts suggesting a threat to a commercial district. The Seventh Circuit described that as a march that was "getting out of hand." Id.

claims.proposition: Even where traffic regulations are validly implicated, the government cannot use them as a pretext to suppress protected political expression.
claims.brief_sentence: Moreover, the Supreme Court confirmed in Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. 536, 554–55 (1965), that even where traffic regulations are validly implicated, the government cannot use them as a pretext to suppress protected political expression.

claims.proposition: Officers may regulate specific individual acts of traffic obstruction without issuing a blanket dispersal order against an entire assembly.
claims.brief_sentence: And as the Seventh Circuit recognized in Thayer v. Chiczewski, 705 F.3d at 246, Chicago officers may regulate specific individual acts of traffic obstruction without issuing a blanket dispersal order against an entire assembly.

claims.proposition: A speculative, subjective concern about potential escalation is constitutionally insufficient to justify dispersing a peaceful assembly.
claims.cited_case: Gregory v. City of Chicago, 394 U.S. 111 (1969)
claims.brief_sentence: The "potential for escalation" is precisely the speculative, subjective concern that Edwards v. South Carolina and Gregory v. City of Chicago held to be constitutionally insufficient.

claims.proposition: Where police protection is ample and a watching crowd is not itself threatening, subjective police concern that a situation might deteriorate does not supply the clear and present danger needed to justify dispersing a peaceful assembly.
quoted_text: ample
claims.brief_sentence: As the Supreme Court made clear in Edwards, police protection is "ample," a watching crowd is not itself threatening, and subjective police concern that a situation might deteriorate does not supply the clear and present danger of serious substantive harm needed to justify dispersing a peaceful assembly. 372 U.S. at 236.

claims.proposition: Feiner involved a speaker who genuinely incited near-riot conditions, with the crowd surging and police authority actively challenged, distinguishing it from an ordinary peaceful protest.
claims.brief_sentence: The Court drew a careful distinction between Feiner v. New York, 340 U.S. 315 (1951) — where a speaker had genuinely incited near-riot conditions, the crowd was surging, and police authority was being actively challenged — and an ordinary peaceful protest attended by verbal disagreements.

claims.proposition: Speech may best serve its high purpose when it induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction, or stirs people to anger.
quoted_text: may best serve its high purpose when it induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger
claims.brief_sentence: The Supreme Court has held that speech "may best serve its high purpose when it induces a condition of unrest, creates dissatisfaction with conditions as they are, or even stirs people to anger." Edwards, 372 U.S. at 237.

claims.proposition: Verbal disagreement is not a fighting-words situation absent actual, face-to-face provocations of an immediate breach of the peace.
quoted_text: fighting words
claims.brief_sentence: Verbal disagreement is not a "fighting words" situation under Chaplinsky v. New Hampshire, 315 U.S. 568 (1942), absent actual, face-to-face provocations of an immediate breach of the peace.

claims.proposition: When police had no arguable probable cause based on the predicate disorderly conduct, arrests for failure to disperse are actionable under § 1983.
quoted_text: the police appear to have had no arguable probable cause
claims.brief_sentence: The Seventh Circuit in Goldhamer v. Nagode found that when "the police appear to have had no arguable probable cause" based on the predicate disorderly conduct, arrests for failure to disperse are actionable under § 1983. 621 F.3d at 591.

claims.proposition: A content-neutral time, place, and manner restriction must be narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest and must leave open ample alternative channels.
claims.cited_case: Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781 (1989)
claims.brief_sentence: Even analyzing the dispersal order as a content-neutral time, place, and manner restriction under Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781, it must be narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest and must leave open ample alternative channels.

claims.proposition: Advocacy of general ideas retains full First Amendment protection unless it is directed to inciting imminent lawless action and is likely to produce such action.
quoted_text: directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action and is likely to incite or produce such action
claims.brief_sentence: The Supreme Court in Brandenburg v. Ohio, 395 U.S. 444, 447 (1969), further established that advocacy of general ideas — including advocacy against government immigration policy — retains full First Amendment protection unless it is "directed to inciting or producing imminent lawless action and is likely to incite or produce such action."

claims.proposition: Police must give notice of a dispersal order and must not begin arresting demonstrators until that notice has been communicated.
claims.brief_sentence: The Supreme Court established in Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. 571, 571–73 (1965), that police must give notice of a dispersal order and must not begin arresting demonstrators until that notice has been communicated.

claims.proposition: Before police can arrest peaceable demonstrators for defying orders, they must communicate the orders to the demonstrators.
claims.cited_case: Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, 747 (7th Cir. 2011)
quoted_text: Before the police could start arresting peaceable demonstrators for defying their orders, they had to communicate the orders to the demonstrators.
claims.brief_sentence: The Seventh Circuit in Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d at 747, expressly applied this rule: "Before the police could start arresting peaceable demonstrators for defying their orders, they had to communicate the orders to the demonstrators."

claims.proposition: Arrests are unlawful where there is no good reason to believe that the arrested protesters, or at least most of them, had heard the orders.
claims.cited_case: Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, 748 (7th Cir. 2011)
quoted_text: good reason to believe that the protesters who were arrested, or at least most of them, had heard the orders
claims.brief_sentence: The court further held that arrests are unlawful where there is no "good reason to believe that the protesters who were arrested, or at least most of them, had heard the orders." Id. at 748.

claims.proposition: Police must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrested person heard, understood, and willfully defied the dispersal order.
claims.cited_case: Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, 748 (7th Cir. 2011)
claims.brief_sentence: Even where a dispersal order is valid, police must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrested person (1) heard the dispersal order, (2) understood it, and (3) willfully defied it. Vodak, 639 F.3d at 748; Barham v. Ramsey, 434 F.3d 565, 573 (D.C. Cir. 2006).

claims.proposition: Police must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrested person heard, understood, and willfully defied the dispersal order.
claims.brief_sentence: Even where a dispersal order is valid, police must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrested person (1) heard the dispersal order, (2) understood it, and (3) willfully defied it. Vodak, 639 F.3d at 748; Barham v. Ramsey, 434 F.3d 565, 573 (D.C. Cir. 2006).

claims.proposition: The fact that some individuals in a group committed criminal acts does not justify mass arrests of others who were peaceably assembled.
claims.cited_case: Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, 748–49 (7th Cir. 2011)
quoted_text: The fact that some of the marchers were rowdy and may have committed criminal acts … need not be discussed in detail; demonstrators' misconduct cannot … be thought to have justified the mass arrests of [others].
claims.brief_sentence: The fact that some individuals in a group may have been disorderly does not supply probable cause to arrest all individuals, including those who were peaceably assembled and had not necessarily received the order. Vodak, 639 F.3d at 748–49 ("The fact that some of the marchers were rowdy and may have committed criminal acts … need not be discussed in detail; demonstrators' misconduct cannot … be thought to have justified the mass arrests of [others].").

claims.proposition: The government cannot entrap citizens by convicting a citizen for exercising a privilege the State had clearly told him was available to him.
claims.cited_case: Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, 746 (7th Cir. 2011)
quoted_text: by convicting a citizen for exercising a privilege which the State had clearly told him was available to him
claims.brief_sentence: [T]he government cannot entrap citizens "by convicting a citizen for exercising a privilege which the State had clearly told him was available to him." Id. at 746 (quoting Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. at 571).

claims.proposition: The government cannot convict a citizen for exercising a privilege the State had clearly told him was available to him.
claims.cited_for: The government cannot convict a citizen for exercising a privilege which the State had clearly told him was available to him.
quoted_text: by convicting a citizen for exercising a privilege which the State had clearly told him was available to him
claims.brief_sentence: [T]he government cannot entrap citizens "by convicting a citizen for exercising a privilege which the State had clearly told him was available to him." Id. at 746 (quoting Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. at 571).

claims.proposition: It would be an indefensible sort of entrapment by the State to allow lawful conduct, then issue a dispersal order, and immediately arrest those who did not comply.
claims.cited_case: Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, 746 (7th Cir. 2011)
quoted_text: an indefensible sort of entrapment by the State
claims.brief_sentence: As the Seventh Circuit noted in Vodak, it would be "an indefensible sort of entrapment by the State" to first allow lawful conduct, then issue a dispersal order, and immediately arrest those who did not comply. 639 F.3d at 746.

claims.proposition: A municipality may be liable under § 1983 through its final policymaking authority.
claims.cited_case: Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)
claims.brief_sentence: [B]oth individual officers and the municipality (through final policymaking authority under Monell v. Dep't of Soc. Servs., 436 U.S. 658 (1978)) may be liable.

claims.proposition: Because Cox established the notice requirement decades before 2003, qualified immunity was unavailable then and a fortiori by 2025.
claims.cited_for: Cox v. Louisiana established the notice requirement decades before 2003, meaning qualified immunity was unavailable then.
claims.cited_case: Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, 750 (7th Cir. 2011)
quoted_text: decades
claims.brief_sentence: See Vodak, 639 F.3d at 750 (Cox v. Louisiana established notice requirement "decades" before 2003, meaning qualified immunity was unavailable then — a fortiori by 2025).

claims.proposition: A political protest in a downtown plaza sits at the zenith of constitutional protection in a traditional public forum.
claims.cited_case: Hague v. Committee for Industrial Organization, 307 U.S. 496, 515 (1939)
claims.brief_sentence: It sits at the zenith of constitutional protection under Hague v. CIO, Edwards v. South Carolina, and Gregory v. City of Chicago.

claims.proposition: A political protest in a downtown plaza sits at the zenith of constitutional protection in a traditional public forum.
claims.brief_sentence: It sits at the zenith of constitutional protection under Hague v. CIO, Edwards v. South Carolina, and Gregory v. City of Chicago.

claims.proposition: A political protest in a downtown plaza sits at the zenith of constitutional protection in a traditional public forum.
claims.cited_case: Gregory v. City of Chicago, 394 U.S. 111 (1969)
claims.brief_sentence: It sits at the zenith of constitutional protection under Hague v. CIO, Edwards v. South Carolina, and Gregory v. City of Chicago.

claims.proposition: As narrowed by Bell v. Keating, a lawful dispersal order under § 8-4-010(d) requires three or more persons in the immediate vicinity committing disorderly conduct likely to cause substantial harm.
claims.brief_sentence: Under Chicago Municipal Code § 8-4-010(d), as narrowed by Bell v. Keating, 697 F.3d 445 (7th Cir. 2012), a lawful dispersal order requires three or more persons in the immediate vicinity committing disorderly conduct likely to cause substantial harm.

claims.proposition: The 'potential for escalation' justification is the type of speculative risk that is constitutionally insufficient.
claims.brief_sentence: The "potential for escalation" justification is precisely the type of speculative risk that Edwards v. South Carolina held is constitutionally insufficient.

claims.proposition: A blanket order to disperse a peaceful protest entirely, rather than targeting individual acts of obstruction, is not narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest.
claims.cited_case: Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781 (1989)
claims.brief_sentence: Even as a content-neutral restriction, the blanket order to disperse a 200-person peaceful protest entirely — rather than narrowly targeting individual acts of actual obstruction — was not narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest under Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781.

claims.proposition: Police must clearly communicate a dispersal order to each individual and must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrestee heard and willfully defied it.
claims.brief_sentence: Under Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. 536, and Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, police must clearly communicate a dispersal order to each individual and must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrestee heard and willfully defied the order.

claims.proposition: Police must clearly communicate a dispersal order to each individual and must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrestee heard and willfully defied it.
claims.cited_case: Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738 (7th Cir. 2011)
claims.brief_sentence: Under Cox v. Louisiana, 379 U.S. 536, and Vodak v. City of Chicago, 639 F.3d 738, police must clearly communicate a dispersal order to each individual and must have individualized probable cause to believe each arrestee heard and willfully defied the order.

claims.proposition: The Brandenburg standard requires speech directed to and likely to produce imminent lawless action, which was nowhere close to being met on these facts.
claims.brief_sentence: The Brandenburg v. Ohio, 395 U.S. 444 (1969), standard — requiring speech directed to and likely to produce imminent lawless action — was nowhere close to being met on these facts.
